item.title: Player's Workbook of Epic Adventures and Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding Review
item.content:encoded:
There's no shortage of books, official or otherwise, that are available for fans of Dungeons & Dragons to take a party on a new adventure, through new settings, using new themes, and with all kinds of new mechanics. While there's a lot that goes into adding onto the basic foundations of the game, I was interested in the release of the Player's Workbook of Epic Adventures and Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding as books that take those basics and expand them to provide the most solid foundation.

These books aren't about how you can min-max your stats the best, come up with unique combinations of abilities, feats, and more to make your DM tear their hair out, or even for the DM on how to introduce new mechanics to trip up or test your players.

Instead, these books are for new and returning players to make sure they understand not just the mechanics of their character, but let them get into the mind of the character. Prompting questions about how they'd interact in different scenarios, or helping them understand the relationships and dynamics between the different characters.

For the DM, it's about making sure the world is understood and as fully fleshed out as it can be, without wasting effort on micro-details that you'll likely never come up against.

The Player's Workbook of Epic Adventures is broken up into a few sections, teaching the player about how to play Dungeons & Dragons in general and how to create their character. These sections aren't a straight guide as you'd find, but a mix of tutorial and open-ended prompting to the player to get an idea of what their ideal version of Dungeons & Dragons looks like.

An immediate example of this is when the book explains, "Before you decide what sort of character you want to create, you need to know what sort of game you're playing." The player is then prompted to ask their DM what the genre of the campaign is, what mood and themes will be present in the story, and what external works the campaign might draw inspiration from.

A player might have the exact happy-go-lucky roguish half-orc, but if the campaign is set in a sci-fi noir setting where the streets are dark, and it's always rainy, there might be a clash. To even account for this, a tips box highlights "If you are using this workbook to develop a character before joining a campaign [...] prepare to make adjustments."

Character Creation Via Quiz

Once a player is aware of the campaign, there are a few pages of quizzes to help them identify which class, sub-class, and background they'll want to pick to get them closest to your ideal character.

Unlike the PHB, which asks "What character do you want to play," the prompting from the book does a lot of heavy lifting to help you understand why you want to play a certain character and how to best align the mechanics of how you want to play with the roleplay that you plan to be performing.

After having understood the campaign and your character, the rest of the book is built around different exercises that can help you inform your character better. There are:

  • Hypothetical scenarios that prompt the player to write how their character would handle a scenario, like a companion getting stuck in a bar brawl, or how you'd locate and identify a mimic
  • How would you handle a scenario based on conflicting alignment
  • Goals and ideals of your character in relation to unanswered questions, or their weaknesses
  • How their magic or abilities might be unique to them through symbols or signature moves

As I mentioned above the work prompted in this book serves as a deeper form of character creation, and also does a lot of the heavy lifting from a Session Zero for the DM to run by providing prompts to characters to not only better understand the type of game they want to be playing in, but also what choices they will be making as a player, and how they'll interact with others.

I think for many players, the mileage may vary for this book. If you're comfortable just knowing how your character handles mechanically, then I doubt you'll have much need for a book like this. But if you're really looking to dive deeper into understanding your character, and putting them in situations that you might not normally see, then this book does a lot.

I'd say for anyone interested, dive into it as deep as you feel comfortable (or can be bothered to), and then you can return at a later time to get further into it.

The one odd aspect of this book is how the blank spaces allow for you to fill out the book and complete it for your character. As someone who has played a handful of characters, and has about a dozen more I'd be interested in playing at sometime I wonder if the book might have been better cutting its page count in half and then directing the player to obtain some kind of character-specific development workbook to write in.

As it is, while I think this is an incredible resource, I don't think I'd ever be able to bring myself to actually write in the book itself when I can reuse its contents over and over again. I know for certain there are a few players at my tables, new and old, that have brought up wanting to get deeper into roleplay, and I'm going to be lending this book out to prompt them with new and fun ideas.

Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding

As a DM who has always run campaigns from Adventure Books, and dabbled in homebrew for one-shots, the Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding is one of the first resources that I've seriously looked at as a way to create your own world and fill it with your creations.

Imitation is said to be the greatest form of flattery, and that's exactly where this book starts. Similarly, to the Player's Workbook, this book is filled with exercises; this time, instead of using it to understand your character and motives, it is to understand your world and build it from the ground up.

"If you're not sure how to approach your campaign setting, look at stories you already enjoy."

First, you highlight worlds that you like and the characters that you enjoy, then, as a flexibility exercise, you're prompted to try putting the characters you like into different worlds and understand how it might alter them.

Before even starting on your own world, you're getting an idea of what you like about the worlds you enjoy, and how you can take elements of characters and themes you like and warp them to better fit a different world.

From there, the building blocks are built upon the world, first with the theme and setting, then to understand the state of the world using broad strokes. How is it to travel? Is magic common? What is the state of technology available to the players and the denizens of the world?

The building blocks continue as you create major settlements, strongholds for allies or enemies, yearly events and customs, cosmology, mysteries, and more.

Once you've got your world, the book then asks the very important question of how do your players fit into the world? It also makes sure to frame it not as a player needing to fit into your game space, but that their choices will influence the world you've created, so it can be a world that has been collaboratively created.

After the world has been built, the book will then promt the DM for how to interact with the party as denizens of the world, or how to set up events for them to be involved in. There's even portions that recommend watching other DMs, in person or through Actual Plays, seeing how they act and react to their players and how you might want to adopt or avoid certain playstyles.

Unlike the Player's Workbook, where I can't imagine writing in it because you'll always have a new character, it's completely the opposite for the Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding. This feels like a powerful tool to set up the basis of your world, to act like its bible.

When you have any second guesses or questions about your own world, you'll be able to return to this tome to double-check what domain that deity is of, or what festival day might be occurring that could serve as a break from the high-stakes action of the campaign.

As a DM who has only ever ran Adventure Books, citing a concern about creativity or storytelling ability as a reason that I haven't attempted to create a homebrew campaign, this book has me considering giving it a try…

Dungeons & Dragons: Player's Workbook of Epic Adventures and Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding Review | Final Thoughts

I think as official resources of how a player can do more to flesh out their character and understand their actions and reactions, and for a DM to do more to create a fleshed out world, these are fantastic companions to have.

I don't believe a lot of the ideas in here are wholly unique, as there are all kinds of training resources out there, but as the ones coming from Wizards of the Coast that new players might first think to pick up, I think they absolutely serve their purpose.

I still do wish the Player's Workbook was smaller with a notepad companion for the variety of characters you might create in the future, I love that the DM's Workbook can be a source of fact about your world and all of the important aspects of it.

If you're a player looking to deepen their roleplay, I'd recommend the Player's Workbook of Epic Adventures, but if you're happy with your current level of character depth, a lot of what's in here might come across as homework or fluff.

For a DM that's interested in branching into a homebrew world and has had concerns about the ideas they're coming up with, how to best structure it, or even trying to figure out where to begin, I think that the Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding might be the exact resource you're after.

Dungeons & Dragons: Player's Workbook of Epic Adventures and Dungeon Master's Workbook of Worldbuilding were reviewed with copies provided by the publisher - All photos were taken over the course of the review



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/players-workbook-of-epic-adventures-and-dungeon-masters-workbook-of-worldbuilding
